page_contents_html:
Hyperkalaemia in adults (primary care): assessment, management and referral

Confirm the result, exclude pseudohyperkalaemia, identify reversible drivers and decide between community management or hospital admission based on severity, ECG and acute illness / AKI status.

  • Immediate admission (999 / ED): any K⁺ ≥6.5 mmol/L; any K⁺ ≥6.0 with ECG changes; mild–moderate hyperkalaemia in an acutely ill patient or with AKI.
  • Community management: mild (5.5–5.9) and moderate (6.0–6.4) when clinically well, no AKI — review medication, diet, treat acidosis, monitor.
  • K⁺ binders (Patiromer / SZC): secondary-care-initiated only, for CKD 3b–5 (not on dialysis) or HF with persistent K⁺ ≥6.0 on sub-optimal RAASi.
  • Repeat testing intervals follow Renal Association / Think Kidneys Table 4.

accordion_sections.heading: Background
accordion_sections.html:
Hyperkalaemia is serum K⁺ >5.5 mmol/L. Severity stratification (UKKA 2023):

  • Mild: 5.5–5.9 mmol/L
  • Moderate: 6.0–6.4 mmol/L
  • Severe: ≥6.5 mmol/L (or any ECG changes / symptoms)

Always exclude pseudohyperkalaemia first. Causes: delayed sample processing (>4 h), haemolysed sample, fist clenching during venepuncture, EDTA contamination, marked thrombocytosis or leucocytosis. If patient is asymptomatic with no obvious cause, repeat the sample urgently before acting.

Common drivers

  • CKD / AKI
  • Drugs: RAASi (ACEi, ARB, MRA, sacubitril/valsartan), potassium supplements, Trimethoprim/co-trimoxazole, NSAIDs, non-selective beta-blockers, heparin, tacrolimus, ciclosporin
  • Salt substitutes ('LoSalt' — almost all are KCl)
  • Addison's disease, hypoaldosteronism
  • Acidosis (DKA, lactic)
  • Tissue breakdown: rhabdomyolysis, tumour lysis, burns

accordion_sections.heading: Assessment
accordion_sections.html:
Sequential assessment with one early decision gate that often determines disposition.

  • Confirm the result. If asymptomatic and no obvious cause, repeat urgently.
  • Decision gate — is the patient acutely ill or has AKI? If YES at any severity (mild or moderate) → refer to hospital. If NO → community management is safe (see Primary Care Management).
  • ECG if K⁺ ≥6.0 or symptomatic — peaked T waves, PR prolongation, wide QRS, sine wave, bradycardia, AV block, VF/asystole.
  • Symptoms (often absent): muscle weakness, fatigue, paraesthesia, palpitations, syncope.
  • Bloods: U&E, eGFR, bicarbonate, glucose, magnesium, FBC; CK if rhabdomyolysis suspected.
  • Drug review: prescribed, OTC, supplements, salt substitutes.
  • Clinical: hydration, BP, urine output, recent illness, AKI risk (D&V, sepsis, contrast).

Pseudohyperkalaemia checklist — before acting on a single result: was the sample sent promptly? haemolysed? fist clenched? known thrombocytosis or leucocytosis? Repeat urgently if any doubt.

accordion_sections.heading: Primary care management
accordion_sections.html:
Follows the UKKA 2023 community algorithm. After confirming the result and excluding pseudohyperkalaemia, the first decision is whether the patient is acutely ill or has AKI — if so, refer to hospital regardless of severity.

SEVERE — K⁺ ≥6.5 mmol/L

  • Refer to hospital immediately (999 / ED) — life-threatening regardless of symptoms.
  • STOP RAASi.
  • Follow Sick Day Rules (hold RAASi, diuretics, NSAIDs, metformin, SGLT2i during acute illness).
  • Continuous cardiac monitoring required in hospital.

MODERATE — K⁺ 6.0–6.4 mmol/L

  • Acutely ill or AKI → refer to hospital.
  • Otherwise → community management as below; repeat U&E within 1 working day (or 24 h if unexpected).
  • If repeat cannot be guaranteed (weekend / OOH) → admit.

MILD — K⁺ 5.5–5.9 mmol/L

  • Acutely ill or AKI → consider hospital referral.
  • Otherwise → community management; repeat U&E within 14 days (or 3 days if unexpected).

A. Review medication — stop or reduce:

  • RAASi: Ramipril / other ACEi, ARBs, Spironolactone, Eplerenone, Sacubitril/Valsartan
  • Potassium supplements (e.g. Sando-K)
  • Trimethoprim / Co-trimoxazole
  • NSAIDs
  • Non-selective beta-blockers (Propranolol, Carvedilol)
  • 'LoSalt' / salt substitutes

B. Lifestyle and supportive measures:

  • Low-K⁺ diet — limit bananas, oranges, tomatoes, potatoes (boil and discard water), dried fruit, nuts, chocolate, avocado. Avoid all salt substitutes.
  • Treat metabolic acidosis — consider Sodium bicarbonate in CKD with low HCO₃⁻.
  • Consider a loop diuretic (Furosemide) to enhance K⁺ excretion if fluid status allows.
  • Treat constipation proactively.

C. CKD stage 3b–5 (not on dialysis) OR Heart Failure

UKKA decision branch:

  • K⁺ persistently ≥6.0 mmol/L AND sub-optimal RAASi therapy → consider secondary-care-initiated K⁺ binder so prognostic RAASi can be continued/optimised.
  • Otherwise → STOP RAASi.

Patiromer ✓ Specialist Initiation Only

Starting dose

8.4 g once daily (max 25.2 g)

Maintenance / titration

Titrate in 8.4 g increments at ≥1-week intervals based on K⁺

Sodium Zirconium Cyclosilicate (SZC / Lokelma) ✓ Specialist Initiation Only

Starting dose

10 g three times daily for max 72 hours

Maintenance

5 g once daily; titrate up to 10 g daily or down to 5 g alternate days

D. Repeat testing intervals (Renal Association / Think Kidneys)

Severity

Clinically well (no AKI)

Unexpected result

Clinically unwell or AKI

Mild (5.5–5.9)

Repeat within 14 days

Repeat within 3 days

Consider hospital referral

Moderate (6.0–6.4)

Repeat within 1 working day*

Repeat within 24 hours

Refer to hospital

Severe (≥6.5)

Refer to hospital for immediate assessment and treatment

*Routine community bloods unavailable at weekends / OOH — admit if interval cannot be guaranteed. (Adapted from Renal Association / Think Kidneys Table 4.)

For SZC and Patiromer: monitor for both efficacy and hypokalaemia.

E. Prevent recurrence

  • Embed RAASi sick-day rules for all CKD/HF patients.
  • Personalised dietary K⁺ counselling for CKD 3b–5.
  • Annual U&E (more frequently in CKD 4–5 or after RAASi titration).
  • Always check for self-prescribed salt substitutes.
  • Treat constipation and metabolic acidosis proactively.

accordion_sections.heading: Who to refer
accordion_sections.html:
999 / ED — immediate admission

  • Any K⁺ ≥6.5 mmol/L.
  • Any K⁺ ≥6.0 mmol/L with ECG changes or symptoms.
  • Mild or moderate hyperkalaemia in an acutely ill patient or with AKI.
  • Suspected Addisonian crisis (hypotension, hyponatraemia, pigmentation).

Same-day medical / renal discussion

  • Moderate K⁺ (6.0–6.4) where community repeat within 24 h cannot be arranged.
  • Rapidly rising K⁺ trend.
  • Moderate hyperkalaemia with concerning AKI risk factors despite 'well' presentation.

Routine renal / cardiology referral

  • CKD 3b–5 (not on dialysis) or HF with persistent K⁺ ≥6.0 mmol/L on sub-optimal RAASi — for K⁺-binder consideration.
  • Recurrent hyperkalaemia despite drug review and dietary measures.
  • Suspected hypoaldosteronism or Addison's (non-acute).

Include in referral

  • Current and previous K⁺ values with trend and dates.
  • Latest ECG.
  • Full medication list including OTC, supplements and salt substitutes.
  • Renal function (eGFR), bicarbonate, AKI status.
  • Comorbidities (CKD stage, HF, diabetes, Addison's risk factors).

accordion_sections.heading: Who not to refer
accordion_sections.html:
Do not refer adults with hyperkalaemia that is appropriate for community management:

  • Confirmed pseudohyperkalaemia on repeat sample.
  • Mild hyperkalaemia (5.5–5.9 mmol/L) in a clinically well patient with no AKI — manage with drug and dietary review and 14-day repeat.
  • Moderate hyperkalaemia (6.0–6.4 mmol/L) in a clinically well patient with no AKI — community management is acceptable provided U&E can be repeated within 1 working day. If this cannot be guaranteed (weekend / OOH), admit instead.
  • Stable chronic mild hyperkalaemia in CKD where the cardiorenal benefit of RAASi outweighs risk and the patient is being monitored.
  • Diet- or supplement-driven mild rise that resolves with advice and removal of the source.

accordion_sections.heading: References
accordion_sections.html:
• UKKA Clinical Practice Guideline: Management of Hyperkalaemia in Adults (Oct 2023, review 2026) — community management algorithm (Appendix 6).
  • Renal Association / Think Kidneys — repeat blood monitoring intervals after a hyperkalaemic episode (Table 4).
  • NICE CKS: Hyperkalaemia
  • BNF: Fluids and electrolytes
